concepts.name: Component Stereotypes
Dependency Injection
concepts.explanation: Instead of your class going out to find its helpers, Spring delivers them to your door. It's like ordering food delivery vs cooking yourself. You just say 'I need a UserRepository' and Spring hands you one. Constructor injection is the recommended way — it makes dependencies obvious and your class easier to test.
keyPoints: Constructor injection is best — makes dependencies explicit and class immutable
@Autowired is optional when you have only one constructor (Spring 4.3+)
Use @Qualifier("beanName") when multiple beans of same type exist
Field injection (@Autowired on fields) works but makes testing harder

concepts.name: @Bean and @Configuration
concepts.explanation: Manually define beans in configuration classes.

concepts.name: @Value and @ConfigurationProperties
REST Controller Basics
concepts.explanation: A REST controller is like a waiter in a restaurant. It takes orders (HTTP requests), talks to the kitchen (service layer), and brings back food (JSON responses). Each HTTP verb has a job: GET fetches, POST creates, PUT updates, DELETE removes.
keyPoints: @RequestBody = 'parse the JSON body into a Java object'

concepts.name: Request Parameters & Headers
concepts.explanation: Handle query parameters and HTTP headers.
keyPoints: Can use Optional<T> for nullable params

concepts.name: Response Status & ResponseEntity
concepts.explanation: Control HTTP response status and headers.
keyPoints: Common statuses: 200 OK, 201 Created, 404 Not Found

concepts.name: Validation
concepts.explanation: Validate request data using Bean Validation.

concepts.name: Exception Handling
CORS Configuration
Basic Security Configuration
UserDetailsService

concepts.explanation: Stateless authentication with JSON Web Tokens.
keyPoints: Add filter to extract token from header

concepts.name: Flyway Migration
concepts.code:
# pom.xml dependency
<dependency>
    <groupId>org.flywaydb</groupId>
    <artifactId>flyway-core</artifactId>
</dependency>

# application.properties
spring.flyway.enabled=true
spring.flyway.baseline-on-migrate=true
spring.flyway.locations=classpath:db/migration

# Create migration files in src/main/resources/db/migration/
# Naming: V1__Initial_schema.sql, V2__Add_users_table.sql

-- V1__Initial_schema.sql
CREATE TABLE users (
    id BIGINT AUTO_INCREMENT PRIMARY KEY,
    username VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL UNIQUE,
    email VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
    created_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
);

-- V2__Add_roles_table.sql
CREATE TABLE roles (
    id BIGINT AUTO_INCREMENT PRIMARY KEY,
    name VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL
);

ALTER TABLE users ADD COLUMN role_id BIGINT;
ALTER TABLE users ADD FOREIGN KEY (role_id) REFERENCES roles(id);

concepts.name: Liquibase Migration
concepts.explanation: Alternative to Flyway with more features.
concepts.code:
# pom.xml dependency
<dependency>
    <groupId>org.liquibase</groupId>
    <artifactId>liquibase-core</artifactId>
</dependency>

# application.properties
spring.liquibase.change-log=classpath:db/changelog/db.changelog-master.xml
spring.liquibase.enabled=true

<!-- db/changelog/db.changelog-master.xml -->
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<databaseChangeLog
    xmlns="http://www.liquibase.org/xml/ns/dbchangelog"
    xmlns:xsi="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema-instance"
    xsi:schemaLocation="http://www.liquibase.org/xml/ns/dbchangelog
                        http://www.liquibase.org/xml/ns/dbchangelog/dbchangelog-4.0.xsd">
    
    <changeSet id="1" author="developer">
        <createTable tableName="users">
            <column name="id" type="BIGINT" autoIncrement="true">
                <constraints primaryKey="true"/>
            </column>
            <column name="username" type="VARCHAR(50)">
                <constraints nullable="false" unique="true"/>
            </column>
            <column name="email" type="VARCHAR(100)">
                <constraints nullable="false"/>
            </column>
        </createTable>
    </changeSet>
    
    <changeSet id="2" author="developer">
        <addColumn tableName="users">
            <column name="phone" type="VARCHAR(20)"/>
        </addColumn>
    </changeSet>
</databaseChangeLog>
keyPoints: Supports XML, YAML, JSON, SQL formats

concepts.name: Building JAR
concepts.code:
# Build with Maven
mvn clean package

# Build with Gradle
./gradlew build

# Run JAR
java -jar target/myapp-1.0.0.jar

# With profile
java -jar -Dspring.profiles.active=prod target/myapp-1.0.0.jar

# With custom port
java -jar -Dserver.port=9090 target/myapp-1.0.0.jar

# pom.xml - Spring Boot Maven Plugin
<build>
    <plugins>
        <plugin>
            <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
            <artifactId>spring-boot-maven-plugin</artifactId>
            <configuration>
                <executable>true</executable>
            </configuration>
        </plugin>
    </plugins>
</build>

concepts.name: Building WAR
keyPoints: Can still run standalone during development

concepts.name: Docker Deployment
concepts.code:
# Dockerfile
FROM openjdk:17-jdk-slim
WORKDIR /app
COPY target/myapp-1.0.0.jar app.jar
EXPOSE 8080
ENTRYPOINT ["java", "-jar", "app.jar"]

# Build image
docker build -t myapp:1.0.0 .

# Run container
docker run -p 8080:8080 myapp:1.0.0

# With environment variables
docker run -p 8080:8080 \
  -e SPRING_PROFILES_ACTIVE=prod \
  -e SPRING_DATASOURCE_URL=jdbc:mysql://db:3306/mydb \
  myapp:1.0.0

# docker-compose.yml
version: '3.8'
services:
  app:
    build: .
    ports:
      - "8080:8080"
    environment:
      - SPRING_PROFILES_ACTIVE=prod
      - SPRING_DATASOURCE_URL=jdbc:mysql://db:3306/mydb
    depends_on:
      - db
  
  db:
    image: mysql:8
    environment:
      - MYSQL_ROOT_PASSWORD=password
      - MYSQL_DATABASE=mydb
    ports:
      - "3306:3306"

concepts.name: Production Configuration
concepts.code:
# application-prod.properties
# Server
server.port=8080
server.shutdown=graceful
spring.lifecycle.timeout-per-shutdown-phase=20s

# Logging
logging.level.root=WARN
logging.level.com.myapp=INFO
logging.file.name=/var/log/myapp.log
logging.pattern.file=%d{yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss} - %msg%n

# Database
spring.datasource.hikari.maximum-pool-size=20
spring.datasource.hikari.minimum-idle=10

# JPA - NEVER use create-drop or create in production!
spring.jpa.hibernate.ddl-auto=validate
spring.jpa.show-sql=false

# Actuator
management.endpoints.web.exposure.include=health,info,metrics
management.endpoint.health.show-details=when-authorized

# Security
management.endpoints.web.base-path=/actuator
management.security.enabled=true

# Compression
server.compression.enabled=true
server.compression.mime-types=text/html,text/xml,text/plain,application/json

concepts.name: @Cacheable
concepts.explanation: Cache method results for better performance.

concepts.name: Logging
concepts.explanation: Logging is your app's black box recorder. Use different levels for different audiences: DEBUG for developers, INFO for operations, WARN for 'hmm, that's odd', and ERROR for 'something broke.' Always use {} placeholders — they're lazy-evaluated!
keyPoints: DEBUG = dev details (hidden in prod), INFO = business events
WARN = 'something's off but we recovered', ERROR = 'something broke'
Use {} placeholders — they skip string building if log level is off
Pass exception as LAST argument: log.error("msg", e) — stack trace included
